_comment: Chronos Earth — Ancient Sites & Precursor-Civilization layer. See public/data/README.md for the schema and a guide to adding your own. 'builtYear' is a signed calendar year (negative = BCE). Markers appear on the globe once the timeline reaches that date. Mainstream consensus is shown first; contested 'alternative' hypotheses (e.g. Graham Hancock) are clearly flagged and never presented as established fact.

sites.name: Göbekli Tepe
sites.consensusSummary: A monumental complex of T-shaped limestone pillars in southeastern Turkey, raised by Pre-Pottery Neolithic hunter-gatherers. It predates pottery, metal tools, writing and farming, overturning the old assumption that monument-building required settled agricultural societies.
sites.significance: The oldest known monumental religious architecture on Earth — built before agriculture, it forced archaeologists to rethink what pre-farming societies could achieve.
keyFacts: Carved T-pillars up to ~5.5 m tall, weighing many tonnes
Built by hunter-gatherers, before pottery or the wheel
Deliberately buried in antiquity, which preserved it
Part of the wider 'Taş Tepeler' cluster of early Neolithic sites

alternative.claim: Hancock cites Göbekli Tepe as evidence that a sophisticated 'lost' culture existed during the last Ice Age and transmitted advanced knowledge to later hunter-gatherers.
alternative.note: Mainstream archaeology attributes the site entirely to local Neolithic foragers and finds no evidence of an earlier advanced civilization. Treat as a contested popular hypothesis, not established fact.

sites.name: Karahan Tepe
sites.consensusSummary: A close 'sister' site to Göbekli Tepe in the Taş Tepeler region, featuring carved pillars and chambers cut into bedrock, including a striking room lined with phallic stone pillars and a carved human head.
sites.significance: Confirms that Göbekli Tepe was not a one-off: an entire early-Neolithic monumental tradition existed in the region around 9500–9000 BCE.
keyFacts: Pillars and rooms carved directly into bedrock
Excavations intensified from 2019 onward
Part of Turkey's 'Taş Tepeler' (Stone Hills) project

sites.name: Çatalhöyük
sites.consensusSummary: A very large Neolithic proto-city in central Anatolia, where mud-brick houses were packed so tightly that residents entered through holes in the roofs and walked across the rooftops. Home to thousands of people for over a millennium.
sites.significance: One of humanity's first large permanent settlements — a window into the transition from villages to urban life, with elaborate wall paintings and household shrines.
keyFacts: Up to ~8,000 inhabitants at its peak
No streets — movement was across the rooftops
The dead were often buried beneath house floors
UNESCO World Heritage Site

sites.name: Nabta Playa
sites.consensusSummary: A prehistoric ceremonial site in the Nubian Desert of southern Egypt, including one of the world's earliest known astronomically aligned stone arrangements — a 'calendar circle' predating Stonehenge by millennia.
sites.significance: Shows sophisticated sky-watching and seasonal timekeeping among cattle-herding desert peoples long before the rise of dynastic Egypt.
keyFacts: Stone circle aligned to the summer solstice and key stars
Built by semi-nomadic cattle herders
Stones later moved to the Nubian Museum in Aswan

sites.name: Stonehenge
sites.consensusSummary: An iconic prehistoric ring of standing stones on Salisbury Plain, England, built and rebuilt in stages over roughly a thousand years. Its giant sarsen stones are aligned with the solstices.
sites.significance: Europe's most famous megalithic monument and a masterpiece of Neolithic engineering, transport and astronomy.
keyFacts: Sarsen stones weigh up to ~25 tonnes each
Smaller 'bluestones' were transported ~250 km from Wales
Aligned to sunrise at the summer solstice
UNESCO World Heritage Site

sites.name: Giza Pyramids
sites.consensusSummary: The pyramid complex on the Giza Plateau, built as royal tombs during Egypt's Fourth Dynasty. The Great Pyramid of Khufu was the tallest human-made structure on Earth for nearly 4,000 years.
sites.significance: The pinnacle of Old Kingdom Egyptian engineering and state organisation, and the only one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World still standing.
keyFacts: Great Pyramid originally ~146 m tall, ~2.3 million blocks
Built by a paid, organised workforce (not slaves)
Aligned to the cardinal directions with great precision
UNESCO World Heritage Site

sites.name: Great Sphinx of Giza
sites.consensusSummary: A colossal limestone statue of a reclining lion with a human head, carved from bedrock on the Giza Plateau. Mainstream Egyptology attributes it to the reign of pharaoh Khafre, around 2500 BCE.
sites.significance: The largest monolithic statue from the ancient world and an enduring symbol of ancient Egypt.
keyFacts: ~73 m long and ~20 m high, carved from a single rock outcrop
Attributed to the Fourth Dynasty (Khafre)
The nose was lost centuries ago; cause debated

alternative.claim: The 'water erosion' hypothesis argues the Sphinx enclosure shows rainfall weathering, implying it was carved far earlier (perhaps 10,000+ BCE), which Hancock links to a lost precursor civilization.
alternative.note: The great majority of geologists and Egyptologists reject this and support the ~2500 BCE date. Presented as a well-known but contested claim.

sites.name: Pumapunku (Tiwanaku)
sites.consensusSummary: Part of the Tiwanaku site near Lake Titicaca in Bolivia, famous for precisely cut andesite and sandstone blocks with crisp right angles and interlocking joints, made by a pre-Inca Andean civilization.
sites.significance: A showcase of advanced Andean stonemasonry and one of the highest major monuments in the ancient world, at ~3,850 m elevation.
keyFacts: Stones cut with remarkable precision and interlocking joints
Built by the Tiwanaku culture, predating the Inca
Radiocarbon dates cluster around the 6th century CE

alternative.claim: Some alternative authors argue the precision stonework implies a much older, technologically advanced lost culture.
alternative.note: Radiocarbon dating firmly places construction in the first millennium CE. The 'lost advanced civilization' reading is not supported by evidence.

sites.name: Gunung Padang
sites.consensusSummary: A megalithic site of columnar basalt arranged in terraces atop a hill in West Java, Indonesia. Mainstream archaeologists date the visible megalithic construction to roughly the first millennium BCE.
sites.significance: Indonesia's largest megalithic site — and a high-profile case study in how extraordinary dating claims get tested and challenged.
keyFacts: Terraces built from natural basalt columns
The underlying hill is a natural volcanic formation
Featured prominently in Hancock's 'Ancient Apocalypse'

alternative.claim: A 2023 paper claimed buried 'construction' as old as ~25,000 years, which Hancock presented as evidence of a lost Ice Age civilization.
alternative.note: That 2023 paper was RETRACTED by the journal in 2024 after experts argued the ancient layers are natural rock, not built structures. A clear example of why dating claims need scrutiny.

sites.name: Younger Dryas Impact Hypothesis
sites.consensusSummary: A proposed cosmic impact or airburst around 12,900 years ago, suggested as a trigger for the sudden 'Younger Dryas' cold snap and the extinction of many Ice Age animals. The marker is placed near Greenland's Hiawatha crater for illustration; the hypothesis concerns a global event.
sites.significance: This event is the cornerstone of Graham Hancock's argument: he proposes it destroyed an advanced Ice Age civilization whose survivors seeded later cultures.
keyFacts: The Younger Dryas cooling itself is well documented
Whether a cosmic impact caused it remains scientifically debated
Marker location is illustrative, not a confirmed impact site

alternative.claim: Hancock argues a comet impact at this time wiped out a globe-spanning advanced civilization, with survivors passing knowledge to hunter-gatherers — explaining sites like Göbekli Tepe.
alternative.note: The 'lost advanced civilization' claim is rejected by mainstream archaeology, and even the impact trigger for the Younger Dryas is contested among scientists. Included because the user asked to feature Hancock's research, clearly labelled as a hypothesis.

sites.name: Richat Structure (Eye of the Sahara)
sites.consensusSummary: A vast bullseye of concentric rock ridges in the Mauritanian Sahara, about 40 km across and clearest from orbit. Geologists read it as a natural landform: a dome of layered rock gently pushed up and then worn flat by erosion, so bands of harder and softer stone stand out as rings. Once thought a meteor crater; there is no sign of an impact, and no sign of a city.
sites.significance: Its uncanny ring-within-ring shape has made it the most popular modern candidate for Plato’s Atlantis — a striking lesson in how ordinary erosion can mimic deliberate design.
keyFacts: ~40 km wide — a landmark for astronauts, an eroded geological dome
Its rings are bands of differing rock hardness, not built walls
No archaeological evidence of a city or civilisation here
Lies west of Egypt, ‘beyond the Pillars of Hercules’ in the old geography
Plato’s Atlantis: alternating rings of water & land, sunk “in a single day and night”
Plato: a plain sheltered by mountains to the NORTH, whose springs fed the rings’ channels

alternative.claim: Plato (Timaeus & Critias) described Atlantis as a central island crowned by a citadel, ringed by ALTERNATING bands of water and land, lying beyond the Pillars of Hercules — an island that in a single day and night “disappeared into the depths of the sea.” The Richat=Atlantis idea reads its concentric rings as exactly those bands, and argues that with a green Sahara and far higher lakes/seas at the end of the Ice Age it could have stood near water and later drowned.
alternative.note: Mainstream scholarship holds that Atlantis is Plato’s ALLEGORY — a moral fable he invented — not a real place; and that the Richat is a natural geological structure with no archaeology of a city, at the wrong scale and elevation for Plato’s account. The real, testable kernel is the drowned-coastline science: seas WERE far lower in the Ice Age and rose after, so genuine coastal sites older than ~10,000 years now lie underwater. Treat the Atlantis link as a contested popular hypothesis, not established fact.

sites.name: Hanging Gardens of Babylon
sites.consensusSummary: A legendary ascending series of planted terraces said to have been raised at Babylon, greenery spilling over stone vaults and watered by machines lifting water from the Euphrates. The only one of the Seven Wonders whose site has never been located, and whose very existence is debated — no Babylonian text mentions it, and some scholars place it at Nineveh instead.
sites.significance: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World — and the most mysterious, surviving only in later Greek and Roman descriptions.
keyFacts: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
Attributed to King Nebuchadnezzar II for his queen Amytis
No archaeological trace has ever been confirmed
Some scholars argue it was really at Nineveh, built by the Assyrian king Sennacherib

sites.name: Temple of Artemis at Ephesus
sites.consensusSummary: A colossal Ionic temple to the goddess Artemis at Ephesus, far larger than the Parthenon, ringed by a double row of some 127 columns around 18 m tall. Burned down in 356 BCE (by an arsonist seeking fame), rebuilt grander still, then destroyed and quarried away in late antiquity. Only a single reassembled column stands on the site today.
sites.significance: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, and one of the largest temples of the classical age.
keyFacts: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
A dipteral temple — a forest of ~127 Ionic columns
Burned in 356 BCE by Herostratus, then rebuilt
Only a lone reconstructed column survives today

sites.name: Statue of Zeus at Olympia
sites.consensusSummary: A ~12 m seated figure of the god Zeus enthroned, made by the sculptor Phidias in gold and ivory over a wooden core (chryselephantine) inside the Temple of Zeus at Olympia. It was said that were the god to stand, he would unroof his own temple. Lost in late antiquity; likely carried to Constantinople and destroyed there by fire.
sites.significance: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, and the masterpiece of the greatest sculptor of classical Greece.
keyFacts: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
Made by Phidias, sculptor of the Parthenon's statues
Chryselephantine — gold robes over ivory flesh on a wooden frame
Held a winged Nike (Victory) in one hand and a sceptre in the other

links.label: Wikipedia: Statue of Zeus at Olympia

sites.name: Mausoleum at Halicarnassus
sites.consensusSummary: The towering tomb of Mausolus, a Persian-era ruler of Caria, at Halicarnassus (modern Bodrum, Turkey): a tall podium carrying an Ionic colonnade, a stepped pyramid roof of 24 steps and, at its summit, a marble four-horse chariot. So famous that his name became the word for any grand tomb — 'mausoleum'. Toppled by earthquakes and quarried by crusaders by the 15th century.
sites.significance: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, and the origin of the word 'mausoleum'.
keyFacts: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
The tomb of Mausolus and his wife Artemisia II
~45 m tall, crowned by a marble chariot
Its name became the word for a grand tomb

sites.name: Colossus of Rhodes
sites.consensusSummary: A ~33 m bronze statue of the sun-god Helios that stood beside the harbour of Rhodes, raised to celebrate the island's survival of a great siege. It stood for only 54 years before an earthquake snapped it at the knees in 226 BCE; the fallen bronze lay admired for centuries. Despite the legend, it did NOT straddle the harbour mouth — it stood upright on one side.
sites.significance: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, and a distant ancestor of the Statue of Liberty in conception.
keyFacts: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
A ~33 m bronze figure of the sun-god Helios
Stood only 54 years before an earthquake felled it in 226 BCE
It stood beside the harbour — the 'straddling the harbour' image is a myth

sites.name: Lighthouse of Alexandria (Pharos)
sites.consensusSummary: The great lighthouse on the island of Pharos at Alexandria, Egypt — over 100 m tall in three stages (a square base, an octagonal midsection and a round lantern) with a fire and mirror guiding ships by night. It stood for over 1,500 years, the last of the ancient Wonders to survive, before earthquakes brought it down between 1303 and 1480. Its very name became the word for 'lighthouse' in many languages.
sites.significance: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, and among the tallest structures on Earth for centuries.
keyFacts: One of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World
Three stages: square base, octagonal middle, round lantern
Guided ships with a fire and a polished mirror
Its name (Pharos) became the word for 'lighthouse' in French, Spanish and Greek
Toppled by earthquakes; blocks have been found on the seabed
